title: Tillich and Barfield
essence: The series concludes with Tillich and Barfield, the final two prophets. Tillich's response to the meaning crisis centers on existential courage — not mere bravery or endurance, but the virtue of seeing through illusion to the good and acting accordingly. His notion of faith as ultimate concern is participatory and aspirational: not asserting propositions but entering into an aspirational trajectory toward the inexhaustible ground of being. For Tillich, God is not a supreme being but an imaginal icon for the ground of being — the simultaneous ground of meaning, reality, and their relationship — such that any attempt to literalize or reify God is idolatry. His method of correlation is not propositional theology but anagogic resonance between the depths of reason (ekstasis) and the depths of reality (miracle as shining and mystery as withdrawal) — coupled together through the symbol. Tillich's existential self/essential self distinction maps precisely onto Vervaeke's sacred second self. Barfield then offers the recovery of participation through poiesis: the ekstasis within creativity, grounded in the Romantic insight (Schlegel/Coleridge) that the finite longs for the infinite. Final participation — recovery of original participation integrated with rational science — requires both Gelassenheit (Heidegger's letting be) and Durchbruch (breakthrough). Vervaeke closes the series by affirming that his relevance realization framework has been put into deep dialogue with all five prophets, yielding a potential synoptic integration.
keyClaims: Tillich's courage to be is an existential virtue involving seeing through illusion — connecting wisdom, religio, and the response to meaninglessness as despair.
Faith as ultimate concern is participatory and aspirational: not propositional assertion but an anagogic trajectory toward the inexhaustible ground of being.

God as the ground of being — an imaginal icon, not a supreme being — survives the critique of ontotheology; literalizing God is idolatry that destroys the symbolic mediation through which gnosis operates.

Barfield's final participation — recovering original participation within the gains of rational science — requires both Gelassenheit (letting be) and Durchbruch (breakthrough), pointing toward the creative poiesis of ekstasis.

The relevance realization framework can enter deep dialogue with all five prophets — Heidegger, Corbin, Jung, Tillich, Barfield — potentially affording a synoptic integration as the response to the meaning crisis.

concepts.summary: Tillich's central concept: existential courage as the virtue of seeing through illusion to the good; contrasted with mere bravery and endurance and connected to the wisdom tradition.
Tillich's God as the ground of meaning, reality, and their relationship — not a supreme being; connecting to Heidegger's critique of ontotheology and Plotinus's the One.

Tillich's imaginal use of the symbol: it participates in what it represents, opens levels of reality and self otherwise closed, is born and dies, and has a surplus of meaning irreducible to propositions.
Tillich's method of correlation is identified as anagogic gnosis: the resonance between ekstasis (depths of reason) and revelation (depths of reality) through the imaginal symbol.
Tillich's method of correlation is explicitly aligned with anagoge: the mutual aspiration of existential questioning and revelation, cycling together in ongoing self-transcendence (epektasis).
Non-theism — rejecting the presuppositions shared by theism and atheism — is the position Tillich arrives at; four shared presuppositions are identified and rejected.
Barfield's final participation involves the ekstasis of creativity — standing beyond oneself in the act of participatory poiesis — as the recovery of transjective connectedness.
Barfield locates sacredness in the poiesis of creativity: the participatory ekstatsis within creative flow that discloses the inexhaustible moreness of reality.
Barfield's final participation is the recovery of their integration; the Romantic infinity-of-reality insight grounds the recovery of participatory contact within scientific logos.

Vervaeke proposes that relevance realization is the cognitive-scientific account of what Tillich calls the depth of reason and Barfield calls participatory knowing — the thread unifying all the prophets.

people.summary: His courage to be, faith as ultimate concern, God as ground of being, method of correlation, idolatry critique, non-theism, and existential/essential self distinction are all examined as the integrative theological prophet.

His final participation, poiesis as ekstasis, Romantic infinity-of-reality insight, and the Gelassenheit-Durchbruch dialectic are examined as the Romantic-poetic prophet who connects creativity and sacredness.
Gelassenheit (letting be) is critiqued as an over-correction; Barfield's Durchbruch (breakthrough) adds the active creative dimension that Heidegger underemphasizes.
Tillich's relationship to Jung: individuation parallels Tillich's tonos between participation and individuation; Tillich deepens Jung by situating individuation within the ground of being.
Barfield's Durchbruch (breakthrough) is connected to Eckhart's own term; Heidegger takes only Gelassenheit from Eckhart while Barfield recovers both.
Tillich uses Nietzsche's will to power as an example of a symbol that bridges subjective and objective; and Nishitani's critique of Nietzsche (he didn't stare into the abyss long enough) is invoked.
Tillich's individuation-participation tonos parallels Buber's I-Thou/I-It distinction; Tillich goes beyond Jung by including the transjective participation dimension Buber emphasizes.
Tillich's essential self as normatively prior to the existential self is aligned with Callard's aspiration: the essential self is normatively primary though temporally secondary.

crossRefs.context: The sacred second self and Jung's individuation — both taken up by Tillich — were developed here.
The WCE framework and the prophets roadmap were introduced here; this episode concludes that roadmap.

quotableMoments.quote: The brave person faces danger, but that's all we can say about them. The person with fortitude endures difficulty, but that's all we can say of them. The courageous person sees through the illusion and the distortion of fear or distress to what is truly good and acts accordingly.
quotableMoments.context: Vervaeke's exposition of Tillich's virtue of courage — connecting it to the wisdom tradition's core of seeing through illusion.
